questions.question: Which article of the Constitution defines India as a Union of States?
questions.explanation: Article 1 states that 'India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States.'

questions.question: The Constitution describes India as:
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as a 'Union of States' — not federation. This implies the union is indestructible and states cannot secede. India is not a true federation.

questions.question: Article 17 abolishes:
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and its practice in any form is an offense punishable by law. The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 and SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 implement this.

questions.question: Article 15 prohibits discrimination on grounds of:
options: Religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth
questions.explanation: Article 15 prohibits discrimination by the State on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, OR place of birth. However, special provisions for women, children, SCs, STs, OBCs are allowed.

questions.question: Cut motions on Demands for Grants are of how many types?
questions.explanation: Three types of Cut Motions: (1) Disapproval of Policy Cut (reduce to ₹1), (2) Economy Cut (reduce by specific amount), (3) Token Cut (reduce by ₹100 to ventilate grievance).

questions.question: Lok Adalats (People's Courts) are established under:
questions.explanation: Lok Adalats are established under the Legal Services Authorities Act 1987 to provide alternative dispute resolution. Awards made by Lok Adalat are deemed decrees and final, not appealable.

questions.question: The Planning Commission was replaced by:
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is an advisory body without power to allocate funds.

questions.question: National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established in:
questions.explanation: National Green Tribunal Act 2010 established the NGT for effective and speedy disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests.

questions.question: Jammu & Kashmir was reorganized into two Union Territories in:
questions.explanation: J&K Reorganization Act 2019 bifurcated the state of J&K into: (1) J&K UT (with legislature), (2) Ladakh UT (without legislature), effective 31 October 2019. Article 370 was also abrogated.

questions.question: Article 371 deals with:
options: Special provisions for certain states (like NE states)
questions.explanation: Articles 371-371J provide special provisions for certain states: 371A (Nagaland), 371B (Assam), 371C (Manipur), 371D-F (Andhra Pradesh/Telangana), 371G (Mizoram), 371H (Arunachal), 371I (Goa), 371J (Karnataka).

questions.question: Which Article prohibits traffic in human beings and forced labour?
questions.explanation: Article 23 prohibits trafficking in human beings, begar (forced labour) and other similar forms of forced labour. Any contravention is an offence punishable by law.

questions.question: Under which Articles are protection against arbitrary arrest and detention provided?
questions.explanation: Article 22 provides protection against arbitrary arrest: right to be informed of grounds, right to consult a lawyer, production before magistrate within 24 hours, and preventive detention limits.

questions.question: How many times has the National Emergency (Art 352) been proclaimed in India?
questions.explanation: National Emergency was proclaimed 3 times: (1) 1962 — Chinese aggression; (2) 1971 — Pakistan war; (3) 1975 — Internal disturbance (the controversial Emergency under Indira Gandhi).

questions.question: NITI Aayog replaced which body in 2015?
questions.explanation: NITI (National Institution for Transforming India) Aayog replaced the Planning Commission on 1 January 2015. Unlike Planning Commission, NITI Aayog is a policy think-tank and does not allocate funds directly to States.

questions.question: India follows the principle of:
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — a person can be a citizen of India only, not of any State. This is unlike the USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship.

questions.question: Article 1 of the Indian Constitution describes India as:
questions.explanation: Article 1 describes India as a 'Union of States' — not a federation. This term was deliberately chosen to indicate that States have no right to secede from the Union (unlike a federation or confederation).

questions.question: The first case of PIL in India was filed by:
options: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979)
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987)
questions.explanation: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979) is considered the first PIL in India, filed by advocate Kapila Hingorani for undertrial prisoners. Justice P.N. Bhagwati was instrumental in developing PIL jurisprudence.

questions.question: The National Commission for Women was established in:
questions.explanation: The National Commission for Women was established under the National Commission for Women Act, 1990 and became operational from 31 January 1992. It reviews constitutional safeguards for women.

questions.question: Article 17 of the Constitution abolishes:
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form. Enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence. The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 implements this.

questions.question: The National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established in:
questions.explanation: The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 established the NGT for effective and speedy disposal of cases relating to environmental protection. It has original, appellate, and settlement jurisdiction in environmental matters.

questions.question: The Gram Sabha consists of:
options: All persons registered as voters in the Panchayat area
All adult residents (whether registered voters or not)
questions.explanation: The Gram Sabha (Art 243A) consists of all persons registered in the electoral rolls of a village — i.e., all adult voters of that village. It is the foundation of democratic decentralization and can exercise powers as the State Legislature decides.

questions.question: The maximum number of members in a State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) cannot exceed:
options: One-third of the total members of the State Legislative Assembly
One-fourth of the total members of the State Assembly
questions.explanation: Article 171 states that the total number of members in a Vidhan Parishad shall not exceed one-third of the total members of the Vidhan Sabha, and shall not be less than 40 members.

questions.question: Which States currently have a Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad)?
options: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh
Only 4 states — UP, Bihar, Maharashtra, Karnataka
questions.explanation: Currently 6 States have Vidhan Parishad: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Bihar, and Uttar Pradesh. Tamil Nadu abolished its Vidhan Parishad in 1986.

questions.question: The principle 'One Vote, One Value' in Indian elections means:
options: Each vote has equal value — no plural voting
Only one person can vote per family
questions.explanation: The principle of 'One Person, One Vote, One Value' ensures universal adult franchise — each citizen above 18 years has one vote of equal value, regardless of wealth, gender, religion, or caste.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Colourable Legislation' relates to:
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Colourable Legislation means that what the legislature cannot do directly, it cannot do indirectly. It is used to examine if a legislature has transgressed its constitutional powers under the legislative lists.

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Severability' (or Separability) in constitutional law states that:
options: Courts can sever an unconstitutional amendment
Valid part of an unconstitutional Act can be saved if separable
States can sever from the Union
High Courts are severed from Supreme Court jurisdiction
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Severability means that if an unconstitutional portion of a law can be separated from the valid portion, only the unconstitutional part is struck down; the rest remains valid.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established in:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established on 12 October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 (amended 2006). Its Chairperson is a retired Chief Justice of India. It investigates human rights violations, studies treaties, and recommends measures for protection.

questions.question: India follows which type of citizenship?
options: Single citizenship (no dual citizenship for states)
questions.explanation: India provides for single citizenship — every citizen is a citizen of India only (unlike USA where citizens have both national and state citizenship). This promotes national unity.

questions.question: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) established:
questions.explanation: Indra Sawhney case (Mandal Commission case, 1992): SC upheld OBC reservation but: (a) 50% ceiling on reservations, (b) Excluded creamy layer from OBC reservation, (c) No reservation in promotions.

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was set up under:
questions.explanation: NHRC was established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. Its Chairperson must be a retired Chief Justice of India. The current Act was amended in 2019.

questions.question: Which Article deals with the official language of the Union?
questions.explanation: Article 343 states Hindi in Devanagari script shall be the official language of the Union. English was also continued as an official language by the Official Languages Act, 1963.

questions.question: Under the 'Emergency Provisions', how many National Emergencies have been declared in India?
questions.explanation: Three National Emergencies have been declared: 1962 (Chinese aggression), 1971 (Pakistani aggression), and 1975-1977 (internal disturbance — the only 'internal' Emergency).

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution protects persons from double jeopardy?
questions.explanation: Article 20(2) provides that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once — protection against double jeopardy.

questions.question: The official language of the Union of India is:
questions.explanation: Article 343: Hindi in Devanagari script is the official language of the Union. English continues to be used for official purposes under Article 343(2). India has no 'national language'.

questions.question: The official language Hindi was to replace English by:
questions.explanation: Article 343(2) provided that English would continue for 15 years until 1965. But due to protests (especially in Tamil Nadu), the Official Languages Act 1963 made English continue indefinitely.

questions.question: Which states in India have a bicameral legislature (two houses)?
options: UP, Maharashtra, Bihar, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, J&K
questions.explanation: States with Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad): UP, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Bihar. J&K had bicameral legislature before Art 370 abrogation.

questions.question: The Right to Information Act was passed in:
questions.explanation: The Right to Information (RTI) Act 2005 was passed to provide citizens access to information held by public authorities, making governance more transparent and accountable.

questions.question: The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act was passed in:
questions.explanation: The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013 established the Lokpal at the Union level and Lokayuktas at the state level for investigation of corruption charges against public servants.

questions.question: Statement 1: Article 51 of the Indian Constitution deals with the promotion of international peace and security. Statement 2: The Parliament has the power to make laws on any matter related to international relations, including treaties and agreements, under Article 246 of the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 51 promotes international peace and security, and Article 246 gives Parliament the power to make laws on international relations.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court's judgment in the Kesavananda Bharati case established the doctrine of basic structure. Statement 2: This doctrine limits the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution in matters related to international relations.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Kesavananda Bharati case established the basic structure doctrine, limiting Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: India is a signatory to the Geneva Conventions. Statement 2: The Parliament of India has enacted laws to implement the provisions of these Conventions.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India is a signatory to the Geneva Conventions and has enacted laws to implement their provisions.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to negotiate and sign treaties and agreements with foreign countries. Statement 2: The President's power to negotiate and sign treaties is subject to the approval of the Parliament.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the President has the power to negotiate and sign treaties, which is subject to Parliament's approval.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of diplomatic relations with foreign countries. Statement 2: The Constitution also provides for the appointment of diplomatic agents to represent India in foreign countries.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Constitution provides for diplomatic relations and the appointment of diplomatic agents.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Parliament of India has the power to declare war or make peace with foreign countries. Statement 2: The power to declare war or make peace is vested in the President of India.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 1 is correct as Parliament has the power to declare war or make peace.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has the power to adjudicate disputes related to international relations. Statement 2: The Supreme Court's power is limited to interpreting the Constitution and does not extend to international relations.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 1 is correct as the Supreme Court has the power to adjudicate disputes related to international relations.

questions.question: Statement 1: India is a member of the United Nations. Statement 2: India's membership in the United Nations is subject to the approval of the Parliament.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 1 is correct as India is a member of the United Nations, but its membership is not subject to Parliament's approval.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the protection of foreign nationals in India. Statement 2: The Constitution also provides for the extradition of foreign nationals who have committed crimes in India.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Constitution provides for the protection of foreign nationals and their extradition.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Government of India has the power to impose economic sanctions on foreign countries. Statement 2: The power to impose economic sanctions is vested in the Parliament of India.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 1 is correct as the Government has the power to impose economic sanctions, not Parliament.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the Executive and the Legislature in the context of international relations: 1. The President has the power to negotiate and sign treaties without the approval of the Parliament. 2. The Parliament has the power to ratify treaties, but this power is not absolute and can be limited by the Executive. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 253 of the Constitution gives Parliament the power to make laws for implementing international treaties, while the Executive negotiates and signs them.

questions.question: Regarding the role of the Supreme Court in international relations, consider the following: 1. The Supreme Court has the power to review international agreements. 2. The Supreme Court can declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional if it violates international law. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has held that it can review international agreements and declare laws unconstitutional if they violate international law or the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the constitutional provisions related to international relations: 1. Article 51 of the Constitution directs the State to promote international peace and security. 2. Article 253 empowers Parliament to make laws for implementing international treaties. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 51 is a Directive Principle of State Policy promoting international peace, while Article 253 gives Parliament the power to implement international treaties.

questions.question: In the context of India's international relations, consider the following: 1. The Prime Minister is the chief representative of India in international forums. 2. The President has ceremonial duties in receiving foreign dignitaries. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister represents India in international forums, while the President has ceremonial duties including receiving foreign dignitaries.

questions.question: Regarding the legislative aspects of international relations, consider: 1. Parliament has the power to declare war. 2. The Executive can unilaterally decide to enter into international agreements without Parliamentary approval. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Parliament has the power to declare war, but the Executive typically negotiates international agreements which may or may not require Parliamentary approval.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about judicial review in international relations: 1. The Supreme Court can review the constitutionality of international treaties. 2. The judiciary has no role in reviewing executive decisions related to foreign policy. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power to review the constitutionality of laws and treaties, limiting the absolute nature of executive decisions in foreign policy.

questions.question: In the context of international relations and the Constitution, consider: 1. Article 73 gives the Executive the power to enter into international agreements. 2. The Parliament must approve all international agreements before they are ratified. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 73 deals with the extent of the executive power of the Union, and while it implies the Executive's role in international agreements, Parliamentary approval is not always required before ratification.

questions.question: Regarding the constitutional provisions for international relations, consider the following: 1. Article 51 directs the State to foster respect for international law. 2. Article 253 enables Parliament to implement international treaties. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 51 is a Directive Principle of State Policy that includes promoting respect for international law, and Article 253 empowers Parliament to make laws for implementing international treaties.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the role of the Executive in international relations: 1. The President represents India in international forums. 2. The Prime Minister is responsible for negotiating international agreements. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: While the President has ceremonial duties, the Prime Minister and the External Affairs Minister are typically involved in negotiating international agreements.

questions.question: In the context of international relations, consider the following statements: 1. The Supreme Court has upheld the principle of sovereign immunity in international law. 2. Parliament has the absolute power to reject international treaties. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has recognized principles of international law, including sovereign immunity, and Parliament has significant powers in relation to international treaties, including their rejection.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the Executive and the Legislature in India: 1. The President of India is not accountable to the Parliament. 2. The Council of Ministers is responsible for advising the President. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The President is accountable to the Parliament, but the Council of Ministers advises the President.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Constitutional provisions for international relations is/are correct? 1. The President represents India in international relations. 2. The Parliament has the power to make laws on international relations.
questions.explanation: The President represents India, and the Parliament makes laws on international relations.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Supreme Court's role in international relations: 1. The Supreme Court has the power to review international agreements. 2. The Supreme Court can declare a law related to international relations as unconstitutional. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court reviews international agreements and declares laws as unconstitutional.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the role of the Executive in international relations is/are correct? 1. The Prime Minister is the head of the Indian delegation to international conferences. 2. The President has the power to negotiate international treaties.
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister leads the delegation, and the President negotiates international treaties.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Constitutional provisions for international relations: 1. Article 51 of the Constitution deals with the promotion of international peace and security. 2. Article 253 deals with the implementation of international agreements. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 51 promotes international peace, and Article 253 implements international agreements.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between the Judiciary and the Executive in international relations is/are correct? 1. The Judiciary has the power to review the Executive's decisions on international relations. 2. The Executive is not bound by the Judiciary's decisions on international relations.
questions.explanation: The Judiciary reviews the Executive's decisions, but the Executive is bound by the Judiciary's decisions.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the role of the Legislature in international relations: 1. The Parliament has the power to approve international agreements. 2. The Parliament can reject a treaty negotiated by the Executive. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Parliament approves international agreements and can reject a treaty.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Constitutional provisions for international relations is/are correct? 1. Article 10 of the Constitution deals with the rights of foreigners. 2. Article 51A deals with the fundamental duties related to international relations.
questions.explanation: Article 51A deals with fundamental duties, including international relations.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Supreme Court's landmark cases on international relations: 1. The Supreme Court has held that international law is part of the law of the land. 2. The Supreme Court has ruled that international agreements are not enforceable in Indian courts. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has held that international law is part of the law, but international agreements are enforceable.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the role of the Executive in international relations is/are correct? 1. The President has the power to receive foreign diplomats. 2. The Prime Minister is responsible for negotiating international treaties.
questions.explanation: The President receives foreign diplomats, and the Prime Minister negotiates international treaties.

questions.question: Statement 1: Article 51 of the Indian Constitution directs the state to promote international peace and security. Statement 2: The Indian government has the power to negotiate and conclude treaties without the approval of Parliament.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 51 promotes international peace and the government can negotiate treaties.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to appoint ambassadors and envoys. Statement 2: The appointment of ambassadors and envoys requires the approval of the Council of Ministers.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the President appoints ambassadors with the advice of the Council of Ministers.

questions.question: Statement 1: India follows a policy of non-alignment in international relations. Statement 2: The policy of non-alignment means that India does not participate in international organizations.
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as India follows non-alignment but participates in international organizations.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to review international agreements. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can declare an international agreement unconstitutional.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Supreme Court can review and declare international agreements unconstitutional.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Parliament has the power to approve or reject international treaties. Statement 2: The Indian government can unilaterally withdraw from an international treaty.
questions.explanation: Neither statement is correct as Parliament's role is limited and withdrawal from treaties is complex.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Ministry of External Affairs is responsible for India's international relations. Statement 2: The Ministry of Defence also plays a role in India's international relations.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as both ministries play roles in international relations.

questions.question: Statement 1: India is a member of the United Nations. Statement 2: India has veto power in the UN Security Council.
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as India is a UN member but lacks veto power.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for diplomatic immunity to foreign diplomats. Statement 2: The Indian government can waive diplomatic immunity in certain cases.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as diplomatic immunity is provided and can be waived.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has upheld the government's power to expel foreign diplomats. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has limited the government's power to expel foreign diplomats.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Court has both upheld and limited this power.

questions.question: Statement 1: India has a consistent policy of recognizing international law. Statement 2: India has ratified several international human rights conventions.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India recognizes international law and has ratified human rights conventions.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the President and the Parliament of India: 1. The President has the power to summon and prorogue the Parliament. 2. The President can dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 85 and 75 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a characteristic of the Indian federal system? 1. Division of powers between the Centre and the States. 2. Independent Judiciary to resolve disputes between the Centre and the States. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the Seventh Schedule and Article 131 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the role of the Supreme Court in inter-state relations: 1. The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction to resolve disputes between States. 2. The Supreme Court can also resolve disputes between the Centre and a State. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 131 and 32 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a provision of the Interstate River Water Disputes Act, 1956? 1. The Act provides for the establishment of a Tribunal to resolve disputes between States. 2. The Tribunal's decision is final and binding on the States. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the Interstate River Water Disputes Act, 1956.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the Centre and the States: 1. The Centre can impose President's Rule in a State under Article 356. 2. The Centre can also deploy Central forces in a State to maintain law and order. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 356 and 355 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a power of the Governor in a State? 1. The Governor has the power to appoint the Chief Minister. 2. The Governor can also reserve a Bill for the President's consideration. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 164 and 200 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the role of the Finance Commission in inter-state relations: 1. The Finance Commission recommends the distribution of taxes between the Centre and the States. 2. The Finance Commission also recommends grants-in-aid to the States. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Article 280 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a provision of the Zonal Councils Act, 1956? 1. The Act provides for the establishment of Zonal Councils to promote cooperation between States. 2. The Zonal Councils can also make recommendations to the Centre on matters of common interest. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per the Zonal Councils Act, 1956.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the Centre and the States in the context of national security: 1. The Centre has the power to deploy Central forces in a State to maintain law and order. 2. The Centre can also impose restrictions on the States in the interest of national security. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 355 and 352 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a power of the President in the context of inter-state relations? 1. The President can summon a joint sitting of the Parliament to resolve a deadlock between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha. 2. The President can also make regulations for the peace and good government of a State under Article 356. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as per Articles 108 and 356 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: Article 51 of the Indian Constitution deals with the promotion of international peace and security. Statement 2: The Parliament has the power to make laws for implementing international treaties under Article 253.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 51 promotes international peace and Article 253 enables Parliament to make laws for international treaties.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court's judgment in the Kesavananda Bharati case established the doctrine of basic structure. Statement 2: The 42nd Amendment Act introduced the concept of socialist and secular in the Preamble of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Kesavananda Bharati case established the basic structure doctrine and the 42nd Amendment introduced socialist and secular in the Preamble.

questions.question: Statement 1: India is a member of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization. Statement 2: The Indian Constitution does not explicitly mention the term 'foreign policy'.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India is a member of SCO and foreign policy is not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to negotiate and sign international treaties. Statement 2: The Parliament's approval is required for the ratification of international treaties.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the President negotiates and signs treaties and Parliament's approval is required for ratification.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of a National Human Rights Commission. Statement 2: The Protection of Human Rights Act was enacted in 1993.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Constitution provides for NHRC and the Protection of Human Rights Act was enacted in 1993.

questions.question: Statement 1: India is a signatory to the Geneva Conventions. Statement 2: The Indian Constitution does not have a specific provision for international humanitarian law.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India is a signatory to the Geneva Conventions and the Constitution lacks a specific provision for international humanitarian law.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to review international agreements under Article 142. Statement 2: The Parliament has the power to enact laws for giving effect to international agreements under Article 253.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Supreme Court can review agreements under Article 142 and Parliament can enact laws under Article 253.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the principle of sovereign equality of states. Statement 2: The United Nations Charter also embodies the principle of sovereign equality of states.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Indian Constitution and UN Charter embody the principle of sovereign equality of states.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Ministry of External Affairs is responsible for the implementation of India's foreign policy. Statement 2: The Indian Constitution does not have a specific provision for the regulation of foreign policy.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the MEA implements foreign policy and the Constitution lacks a specific provision for its regulation.

questions.question: Statement 1: India has ratified the Paris Agreement on climate change. Statement 2: The Indian Constitution provides for the protection of the environment under Article 51-A.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India has ratified the Paris Agreement and the Constitution provides for environmental protection under Article 51-A.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to negotiate and conclude treaties and agreements with foreign countries under Article 253 of the Constitution. Statement 2: The Parliament's approval is required for the ratification of treaties and agreements.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the President negotiates and concludes treaties, and Parliament's approval is required for ratification.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of diplomatic relations with foreign countries under Article 51. Statement 2: The Ministry of External Affairs is responsible for the implementation of India's foreign policy.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, Article 51 promotes international relations and the Ministry of External Affairs implements foreign policy.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Parliament has the power to declare war or conclude peace under Article 246 of the Constitution. Statement 2: The President has the power to receive foreign diplomats and send Indian diplomats to foreign countries.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 2 is correct, the President receives foreign diplomats and sends Indian diplomats.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court's judgment in the Ram Jawaya Kapur case established the principle of parliamentary sovereignty. Statement 2: This principle limits the power of the judiciary to review laws related to international relations.
questions.explanation: Only Statement 1 is correct, the Ram Jawaya Kapur case established parliamentary sovereignty.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the protection of foreign nationals in India under Article 21. Statement 2: The government has the power to expel foreign nationals from India under the Foreigners Act, 1946.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, Article 21 protects foreign nationals and the government can expel them under the Foreigners Act.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Parliament has the power to impose restrictions on the freedom of speech and expression in the interest of friendly relations with foreign states under Article 19. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has upheld the validity of such restrictions in the Romesh Thapar case.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, Parliament can impose restrictions and the Supreme Court upheld them in the Romesh Thapar case.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of international organizations under Article 51. Statement 2: India is a member of the United Nations and participates in its activities.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, Article 51 promotes international organizations and India is a UN member.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to grant diplomatic recognition to foreign states under Article 253 of the Constitution. Statement 2: The government has the power to enter into international agreements and treaties without parliamentary approval.
